title: Discharge Teaching for a 68-Year-Old Woman with NEWLY DIAGNOSED Nonvalvular Atrial Fibrillation Started on the DIRECT ORAL ANTICOAGULANT (DOAC) APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID for Stroke Prevention (CHA2DS2-VASc 4 = High Stroke Risk): WHY Lifelong Anticoagulation, Exact Dose/Timing and the DOAC-Specific MISSED-DOSE Rule, the Key Distinctions from WARFARIN (NO Routine INR, NO Vitamin K Dietary Restriction), Drug/Food Interactions (NSAID AVOIDANCE; CYP3A4 + P-gp Inhibitors/Inducers), Bleeding Precautions and the SIGNS of Bleeding to Report, Perioperative Holds, Reversal Availability (Andexanet Alfa), Medical-Alert ID, and ADHERENCE/Follow-Up
subtitle: DIRECT ORAL ANTICOAGULANTS (DOACs) — apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran — have largely replaced warfarin for stroke prevention in NONVALVULAR ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (NVAF) and for treatment of venous thromboembolism. They have predictable pharmacokinetics, do NOT require routine coagulation (INR/PT) monitoring, and have FEW food interactions — a major shift from warfarin. NURSING TEACHING is the heart of safe DOAC use, because ADHERENCE and BLEEDING SAFETY are entirely patient-driven. INDICATION here: a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 (age 65-74 = 1, female sex = 1, hypertension = 1, diabetes = 1) places her at HIGH annual stroke risk; anticoagulation is indicated for stroke prevention and is LIFELONG unless AFib is permanently eliminated. APIXABAN is a direct factor Xa inhibitor; the standard NVAF dose is 5 mg PO BID; DOSE REDUCTION to 2.5 mg BID applies if she meets 2 of 3: age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg, serum creatinine >= 1.5 mg/dL (she meets none and stays on 5 mg BID). DOSING/TIMING: BID, about 12 hours apart, with or without food, with water. MISSED DOSE (high yield and DOAC-specific): if remembered on the SAME day, take it as soon as remembered and continue the schedule; if she misses it and it is already CLOSE to the next dose (the next day for a BID schedule), SKIP the missed dose and resume — NEVER DOUBLE UP. MONITORING is KEY DIFFERENCE FROM WARFARIN: NO routine INR/PT — predictable PK means no titration; instead obtain periodic CBC and renal/hepatic function (apixaban dosing follows renal function; significant changes in renal function may shift dose). INTERACTIONS: AVOID NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) and aspirin/other antiplatelets unless directed — additive GI/intracranial bleeding risk; use ACETAMINOPHEN for pain. STRONG dual CYP3A4 + P-glycoprotein INHIBITORS (e.g., ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, clarithromycin) RAISE apixaban levels and bleeding risk and require dose adjustment or avoidance. STRONG dual CYP3A4 + P-gp INDUCERS (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, ST JOHN'S WORT) LOWER apixaban levels and reduce stroke protection — avoid. Avoid combining with other anticoagulants. DIET: there is NO vitamin K restriction with DOACs (unlike warfarin) — she may eat normal amounts of leafy greens (broccoli, spinach, kale); a frequent over-teaching error is applying warfarin dietary rules to DOACs. ALCOHOL: moderation, since heavy use raises bleeding risk. BLEEDING PRECAUTIONS: soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid contact sports, watch for falls; carry a MEDICAL-ALERT ID and a wallet card stating she takes apixaban. SIGNS OF BLEEDING to report immediately — overt: nosebleed that will not stop, blood in urine (pink/red), black-tarry or bloody stool, vomiting blood or 'coffee grounds,' heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding, unusual bruising; OCCULT/SERIOUS: severe headache, sudden severe abdominal or back pain, sudden weakness/numbness, change in vision, dizziness, fainting (possible intracranial or retroperitoneal hemorrhage). PERIOPERATIVE: do NOT stop without her provider's direction; planned procedures require provider-coordinated holds based on procedure bleeding risk and her renal function (typically 24-48 hours for moderate risk; longer for higher risk or impaired renal function); inform ALL providers (dentists, surgeons, ED, gastroenterology) that she takes apixaban. REVERSAL/EMERGENCIES: andexanet alfa is the specific reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban) in life-threatening or uncontrolled bleeding; 4F-PCC may be used when andexanet alfa is unavailable; if she has a fall with head strike, severe bleeding, or signs of intracranial bleed, she should go to the ED immediately and state she takes apixaban. ADHERENCE is the single most important predictor of effectiveness — missed doses lose stroke protection within HOURS due to short half-life; encourage a pill organizer/phone reminder, keep refills stocked, and explore cost/insurance/assistance programs (DOACs are expensive — non-adherence due to cost is common and dangerous). PREGNANCY: not recommended; counsel women of childbearing potential. CARDIOVERSION nuance: if AFib has been present >48 hours (or unknown duration), 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation is required BEFORE elective cardioversion (or TEE to exclude left-atrial thrombus). FOLLOW-UP: lifelong anticoagulation while AFib persists; reassess CHA2DS2-VASc and HAS-BLED periodically; periodic CBC/renal/hepatic labs; do NOT self-discontinue. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: applying warfarin's vitamin-K dietary rules to a DOAC; taking ibuprofen/aspirin for routine pain; doubling up after a missed dose; stopping the drug whenever she 'feels fine' (AFib is often asymptomatic, but stroke risk persists); not informing dentists/surgeons; not carrying medical-alert ID. NURSING ROLE: teach the WHY (NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc → lifelong stroke prevention), the exact regimen and DOAC-specific missed-dose rule, the WARFARIN-vs-DOAC distinctions (no routine INR, no vitamin K restriction), interactions (NSAID avoidance, CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitors/inducers), bleeding precautions and the signs to report, perioperative coordination and reversal awareness, and a robust adherence plan — verified by TEACH-BACK with the patient and family.

patient.name: E. Wagner
patient.code: Outpatient cardiology/primary-care follow-up; full code; goal = comprehensive, teach-back-verified discharge teaching for a new high-alert DOAC (apixaban) for stroke prevention in nonvalvular AFib — adherence, regimen and missed-dose rule, warfarin-vs-DOAC distinctions (no routine INR, no vitamin K restriction), interactions, bleeding precautions, perioperative coordination, reversal awareness, medical-alert ID, and follow-up
patient.admitDate: Newly diagnosed NONVALVULAR ATRIAL FIBRILLATION on a clinic ECG done for palpitations and fatigue; rate-controlled, currently in AFib. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes (well controlled on metformin), osteoarthritis (uses occasional ibuprofen). CHA2DS2-VASc = 4 (age 65-74, female sex, HTN, DM) = HIGH stroke risk → anticoagulation indicated. HAS-BLED reviewed and modifiable risk factors addressed. Provider initiated APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (she does NOT meet dose-reduction criteria: age 68, weight 72 kg, SCr 0.9). She lives alone, is health-literate, and is anxious about bleeding. Today's visit focuses on COMPREHENSIVE DOAC DISCHARGE TEACHING with teach-back, including a hard stop on her routine ibuprofen use.
patient.room: Outpatient cardiology/primary-care clinic; interdisciplinary support (cardiology, primary care, clinic RN, pharmacy/MTM, patient educator); follow-up scheduled

labs.n: PRESENTATION + indication for DOAC anticoagulation in NVAF
labs.v: 68 y/o woman E. Wagner with newly diagnosed NONVALVULAR ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (clinic ECG), HTN, T2DM, osteoarthritis (routine ibuprofen — to STOP). CHA2DS2-VASc = 4 (age 65-74 = 1, female sex = 1, HTN = 1, DM = 1) = HIGH annual stroke risk → anticoagulation indicated for stroke prevention; HAS-BLED reviewed and modifiable risk factors addressed. Provider chose APIXABAN — a direct factor Xa inhibitor — over warfarin for predictable PK, no routine INR monitoring, fewer food interactions, and a favorable safety profile in many patients. Dose: APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID; she does NOT meet dose-reduction criteria (would require 2 of: age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg, SCr >= 1.5 — she is 68, 72 kg, SCr 0.9). Anticoagulation will be LIFELONG while AFib persists. Today's nurse focus: comprehensive DOAC discharge teaching with TEACH-BACK and a robust ADHERENCE/safety plan — and stopping her routine ibuprofen.
labs.r: NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc 4 = HIGH stroke risk → lifelong anticoagulation. APIXABAN (direct factor Xa inhibitor) 5 mg PO BID standard NVAF dose; dose-reduce to 2.5 BID ONLY if 2 of (age >=80, wt <=60 kg, SCr >=1.5) — she meets none. HAS-BLED guides modifiable risk reduction, not avoidance of needed anticoagulation. RN role: teach the WHY + comprehensive DOAC plan + stop the ibuprofen.

labs.v: APIXABAN inhibits factor Xa, blocking thrombin generation. DOSE/TIMING: 5 mg PO BID about 12 hours apart, with or without food, with water. MISSED-DOSE RULE (high yield, DOAC-specific): if remembered on the SAME day, take as soon as remembered and continue the schedule; if it is close to the next dose (the next day on a BID schedule), SKIP and resume — DO NOT DOUBLE UP. MONITORING — the KEY DIFFERENCES FROM WARFARIN: (1) NO routine INR/PT — predictable PK; (2) obtain periodic CBC and renal/hepatic function; (3) NO vitamin K dietary restriction (apixaban is not vitamin-K dependent). The DOAC HALF-LIFE is short (about 12 hours for apixaban) — missed doses lose stroke protection within hours, so adherence and refills are critical. Compared to warfarin: predictable, no INR titration, fewer food interactions, faster onset/offset, but higher acquisition cost and renal-dose-dependent; specific reversal exists for life-threatening bleeding.
labs.r: Apixaban = direct factor Xa inhibitor; 5 mg PO BID ~12h apart, w/ or w/o food. MISSED DOSE: same day = take when remembered; close to next dose = SKIP — NEVER DOUBLE. NO routine INR/PT (predictable PK); periodic CBC + renal/hepatic; NO vitamin K diet restriction. Short half-life → missed doses lose protection within hours → adherence + refills critical. Versus warfarin: predictable, no INR, fewer food interactions, faster on/off, costly, renal-dose-dependent, specific reversal available. RN role: regimen + missed-dose + warfarin-vs-DOAC distinctions.

labs.n: Drug/food interactions + bleeding precautions + signs to report
labs.v: INTERACTIONS: AVOID NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, ketorolac) and aspirin/other antiplatelets unless specifically directed — additive GI/intracranial bleeding risk; use ACETAMINOPHEN for routine pain. AVOID concurrent other anticoagulants. Strong DUAL CYP3A4 + P-glycoprotein INHIBITORS (e.g., ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, clarithromycin) RAISE apixaban levels and bleeding risk — avoid or dose-adjust. Strong DUAL CYP3A4 + P-gp INDUCERS (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, ST JOHN'S WORT) LOWER apixaban levels and stroke protection — avoid. Tell the provider/pharmacist BEFORE starting any new prescription, OTC product, or herbal/supplement. DIET: NORMAL diet; NO vitamin K restriction (a warfarin rule, NOT a DOAC rule); ALCOHOL in moderation. BLEEDING PRECAUTIONS: soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid contact sports/high-fall-risk activities, fall prevention at home, carry MEDICAL-ALERT ID + wallet card identifying apixaban. SIGNS OF BLEEDING to REPORT immediately — OVERT: nosebleed that will not stop, pink/red urine, black-tarry or bloody stool, vomiting blood or 'coffee grounds,' heavy or prolonged menses, unusual bruising; URGENT/possible internal hemorrhage: severe headache, sudden severe abdominal or back pain, sudden weakness/numbness, vision change, dizziness, fainting → call 911 / go to the ED and state she takes APIXABAN.
labs.r: AVOID NSAIDs + aspirin/antiplatelets (use ACETAMINOPHEN); avoid other anticoagulants. CYP3A4 + P-gp INHIBITORS raise levels (azoles/clarithromycin/ritonavir); INDUCERS lower levels (rifampin/phenytoin/carbamazepine/ST JOHN'S WORT) — call provider before any new med/supplement. NORMAL diet (NO vit K restriction); moderate alcohol. Precautions: soft toothbrush + electric razor + avoid contact sports + fall prevention + MEDICAL-ALERT ID + wallet card. Report overt bleeding (nose/urine/stool/vomit/menses/bruising) + URGENT possible internal bleed (severe HA, severe abd/back pain, sudden weakness/vision/dizziness) → 911/ED. RN role: interactions + precautions + signs to report.

labs.n: Perioperative + reversal + adherence + follow-up + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PERIOPERATIVE: do NOT stop apixaban without provider direction; planned procedures require a provider-coordinated HOLD based on the procedure's bleeding risk and her renal function (typically 24-48 hours for moderate-risk procedures; longer for higher-risk procedures or impaired renal function); inform ALL providers (dentists, surgeons, gastroenterology, ophthalmology, ED) that she takes apixaban; arrange a bridging plan only if specifically prescribed (most low-risk DOACs do not require bridging). REVERSAL: for life-threatening or uncontrolled bleeding, ANDEXANET ALFA is the specific reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban); 4F-PCC may be used when andexanet alfa is unavailable; idarucizumab is for dabigatran (a thrombin inhibitor) — not apixaban. ADHERENCE is the single most important predictor of effectiveness — missed doses can lose stroke protection within hours; use a pill organizer, phone reminders, link doses to existing routines, keep refills filled, and address COST/insurance/assistance programs (DOACs are expensive; non-adherence due to cost is common and dangerous — never skip due to cost without contacting the provider; switching back to warfarin is preferable to stopping). FOLLOW-UP: lifelong anticoagulation while AFib persists; periodic CBC and renal/hepatic labs; reassess CHA2DS2-VASc and HAS-BLED periodically; do NOT self-discontinue when she 'feels fine' (AFib is often asymptomatic but stroke risk persists); cardioversion nuance — if AFib >48 hours or unknown duration, requires 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation OR a TEE before elective cardioversion. PREGNANCY: not recommended; counsel women of childbearing potential. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct teaching): the nurse did NOT apply warfarin's vitamin-K dietary rules to a DOAC; did NOT tell her to keep using ibuprofen for arthritis; did NOT tell her to double up after a missed dose; did NOT tell her she could stop the medication when 'feeling fine'; did NOT skip the discussion of medical-alert ID and informing other providers. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: understanding of indication + adherence plan + correct missed-dose rule + NSAID stop + correct interaction list + normal diet (no false restriction) + bleeding precautions + signs to report + perioperative coordination + reversal/ED awareness + medical-alert ID + cost/refill plan + lifelong follow-up + family informed + teach-back verified. EXPECTED outcome: consistent adherence, no major bleeding, no stroke, no perioperative bleeding event, sustained protection against thromboembolic stroke.
labs.r: Perioperative: don't self-stop; provider-coordinated hold ~24-48h (longer for high bleed risk/poor renal); inform ALL providers/dentists/ED; bridging only if specifically prescribed. REVERSAL: ANDEXANET ALFA for FXa inhibitor (apixaban) bleeding; 4F-PCC if unavailable; idarucizumab is for DABIGATRAN, not apixaban. ADHERENCE = #1 predictor (short t1/2 → protection lost in hours); pill organizer/reminders/refills/COST plan; never skip due to cost without provider. FOLLOW-UP lifelong while AFib persists; periodic CBC + renal/hepatic; cardioversion needs 3 wks anticoag OR TEE if AFib >48h. Negatives confirm correct practice (no warfarin diet rules, no ibuprofen, no doubling, no self-stop, medical-alert ID). Protective: indication understood + adherence + correct missed-dose + NSAID stop + correct interactions + normal diet + bleeding precautions + signs to report + perioperative coordination + reversal awareness + medical-alert ID + cost/refill + follow-up + family + teach-back.

labs.v: 0.9 mg/dL (does not trigger apixaban dose reduction; >= 1.5 would be one of the three reduction criteria)
78 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (preserved renal function; supports standard 5 mg BID dosing)
13.2 g/dL (baseline; trend with periodic CBC during therapy)

nursesNote: Outpatient clinic — 68 y/o woman, newly dx NONVALVULAR AFib (clinic ECG), HTN/T2DM/OA; CHA2DS2-VASc 4 = HIGH stroke risk → anticoagulation indicated; HAS-BLED reviewed. Started on APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (no dose-reduction criteria met: age 68, 72 kg, SCr 0.9). Lives alone, health-literate, anxious about bleeding, was taking routine ibuprofen — HARD STOP today (use acetaminophen). COMPREHENSIVE DOAC TEACHING delivered: (1) WHY — NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc 4 → lifelong stroke prevention; AFib often asymptomatic but stroke risk persists, so DON'T stop when feeling fine. (2) REGIMEN — 5 mg PO BID ~12h apart, w/ or w/o food, water. MISSED DOSE: same day = take when remembered; close to next dose = SKIP — NEVER DOUBLE. (3) WARFARIN-vs-DOAC distinctions — NO routine INR/PT (predictable PK); periodic CBC + renal/hepatic; NO vitamin K dietary restriction (normal diet); short t1/2 → adherence + refills critical. (4) INTERACTIONS — AVOID NSAIDs + aspirin/antiplatelets (use ACETAMINOPHEN); avoid other anticoagulants; CYP3A4 + P-gp INHIBITORS raise levels (azoles/clarithromycin/ritonavir); INDUCERS lower levels (rifampin/phenytoin/carbamazepine/ST JOHN'S WORT); call provider/pharmacist BEFORE any new med/supplement. (5) BLEEDING PRECAUTIONS + SIGNS — soft toothbrush + electric razor + no contact sports + fall prevention + MEDICAL-ALERT ID + wallet card. Report: nose bleed that won't stop, pink/red urine, black-tarry/bloody stool, vomit blood or coffee grounds, heavy/prolonged menses, unusual bruising. URGENT (possible intracranial/retroperitoneal bleed) — severe HA, severe abd/back pain, sudden weakness/numbness, vision change, dizziness/fainting → 911/ED and state APIXABAN. (6) PERIOPERATIVE — don't self-stop; provider-coordinated hold ~24-48h (longer if higher risk/poor renal); inform ALL providers/dentists/surgeons/ED. REVERSAL = ANDEXANET ALFA for FXa-inhibitor bleeding (4F-PCC if unavailable; idarucizumab is for DABIGATRAN, NOT apixaban). (7) ADHERENCE = #1 predictor — pill organizer + phone reminder set up; refills + cost/assistance discussed; family informed. FOLLOW-UP: lifelong anticoagulation while AFib persists; periodic CBC + renal/hepatic; cardioversion nuance (AFib >48h needs 3 wks anticoag or TEE). NEGATIVES (correct teaching): did NOT apply warfarin vit-K rules to DOAC, did NOT continue ibuprofen, did NOT teach doubling for missed dose, did NOT say 'stop when you feel fine,' did NOT skip medical-alert ID / provider notification. TEACH-BACK: verbalizes 'apixaban 5 mg every 12 hours, no ibuprofen, acetaminophen for pain, no double dose if missed, tell every doctor/dentist, 911 for severe headache/weakness/bleeding.' Follow-up scheduled.
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS: new nonvalvular atrial fibrillation; CHA2DS2-VASc 4 (high stroke risk); HAS-BLED reviewed; rate-controlled
ANTICOAGULATION: apixaban 5 mg PO BID for stroke prevention; does not meet dose-reduction criteria; lifelong while AFib persists; do not self-discontinue
AVOID: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, ketorolac), aspirin, and other antiplatelets unless specifically directed; avoid other anticoagulants; use acetaminophen for routine pain

CYP3A4 + P-gp INTERACTIONS: notify provider/pharmacist BEFORE starting any new prescription, OTC product, or herbal/supplement (e.g., azole antifungals, clarithromycin, ritonavir, rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St. John's wort)
MONITORING: NO routine INR/PT; obtain periodic CBC and renal/hepatic function; reassess CHA2DS2-VASc and HAS-BLED periodically
DIET/LIFESTYLE: normal diet with NO vitamin K restriction; moderate alcohol; fall prevention; carry medical-alert ID and a wallet card noting apixaban

BLEEDING PRECAUTIONS + WHAT TO REPORT: soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid contact sports; report overt bleeding (nose, urinary, GI, menstrual, bruising) and call 911/go to ED for severe headache, severe abdominal/back pain, sudden weakness/numbness, vision change, dizziness, or fainting — state APIXABAN

PERIOPERATIVE: do not stop apixaban without provider direction; for planned procedures, provider-coordinated hold (typically 24-48 hours; longer for higher bleed risk or impaired renal function); inform all providers, dentists, and ED that she takes apixaban
REVERSAL/EMERGENCIES: andexanet alfa for life-threatening factor Xa inhibitor bleeding (4F-PCC if unavailable); idarucizumab is specific to dabigatran (not apixaban)

ADHERENCE: pill organizer + phone reminder; maintain refills; address cost/insurance/assistance — never skip due to cost without contacting the provider (switch to warfarin if needed); women of childbearing potential — DOAC not recommended in pregnancy

FOLLOW-UP: clinic follow-up in 4 weeks; cardioversion nuance (AFib >48 hours or unknown duration requires 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation OR a TEE before elective cardioversion); teach-back at every contact

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this new-DOAC teaching visit?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: New NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc 4 (high stroke risk) → lifelong anticoagulation indicated; apixaban (a direct factor Xa inhibitor) was started
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the indication and lifelong-protection mindset anchor adherence teaching. RN role: teach the WHY.

opts.text: Apixaban 5 mg PO BID about 12 hours apart, with or without food; dose-reduction criteria (2 of: age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg, SCr >= 1.5) — none met here
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct dose and why it is not reduced anchors safe dosing. RN role: teach the regimen.

opts.text: DOAC missed-dose rule: same day = take when remembered; close to next dose = SKIP, NEVER DOUBLE
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DOAC-specific and high-yield; doubling causes bleeding, skipping forever loses protection. RN role: teach missed-dose.

opts.text: NO routine INR/PT (predictable PK); periodic CBC + renal/hepatic — and NO vitamin K dietary restriction (a warfarin rule, NOT a DOAC rule)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — warfarin-vs-DOAC distinctions correct over-teaching errors. RN role: teach the distinctions.

opts.text: Avoid NSAIDs (her routine ibuprofen) and aspirin/other antiplatelets; use acetaminophen — and call before any new med/supplement because of CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitor/inducer interactions
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the most actionable interaction risk; ibuprofen is a hard stop. RN role: stop NSAIDs + interactions.

opts.text: Bleeding precautions + signs to report (overt and possible internal); perioperative coordination; ANDEXANET ALFA for life-threatening reversal; medical-alert ID; cost/refill/adherence plan
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the safety net that turns a prescription into safe long-term care. RN role: precautions + reporting + coordination + adherence.

opts.text: Tell her to strictly avoid all leafy green vegetables (spinach, kale, broccoli) because they will counteract the apixaban
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Vitamin K dietary restriction applies to WARFARIN, not to DOACs; apixaban has no vitamin K-dependent mechanism.

opts.text: Continue her routine ibuprofen for arthritis and, if she misses a dose of apixaban, just take a double dose to catch up
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. NSAIDs add bleeding risk and must stop; doubling a missed dose causes bleeding — same day take when remembered, otherwise SKIP.

opts.text: NVAF stroke risk is stratified by CHA2DS2-VASc; HAS-BLED estimates bleeding risk and guides modifiable risk reduction, not avoidance of needed anticoagulation
opts.rat: CORRECT — the risk-stratification framework. Foundation for indication and counseling.

opts.text: Apixaban is a direct factor Xa inhibitor with predictable pharmacokinetics; standard NVAF dose is 5 mg PO BID with dose reduction to 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3 criteria are met (age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg, SCr >= 1.5)
opts.rat: CORRECT — class and dosing rule. Foundation for correct administration.

opts.text: DOACs do not require routine INR/PT monitoring; periodic CBC and renal/hepatic labs are obtained instead, and there is no vitamin K dietary restriction
opts.rat: CORRECT — monitoring and diet principles. Foundation for correcting warfarin over-teaching.

opts.text: NSAIDs/aspirin/other antiplatelets and other anticoagulants increase bleeding risk; strong dual CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitors raise apixaban levels and inducers lower them
opts.rat: CORRECT — interaction principles. Foundation for safe co-prescribing.

opts.text: Missed-dose rule: same day = take as soon as remembered; close to the next dose = skip and resume; never double up
opts.rat: CORRECT — DOAC-specific missed-dose rule. Foundation for adherence.

opts.text: Andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban) in life-threatening bleeding; 4F-PCC may be used when andexanet alfa is unavailable; idarucizumab is specific to dabigatran
opts.rat: CORRECT — reversal principles. Foundation for emergency awareness.

opts.text: Apixaban requires weekly INR monitoring and dose titration, just like warfarin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. DOACs do not require routine INR/PT; their predictable PK obviates titration.

opts.text: Vitamin K-rich foods (spinach, kale, broccoli) must be strictly avoided on a DOAC because they will reduce its effect
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Vitamin K restriction is a warfarin rule. DOACs do not depend on vitamin K and have no vitamin K dietary restriction.

opts.text: Ensure she understands WHY (NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc 4 → lifelong stroke prevention) and that ADHERENCE is non-negotiable
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Without the WHY and adherence, nothing else holds; AFib is often asymptomatic but stroke risk persists. RN role: WHY + adherence.

opts.text: Teach the regimen (5 mg PO BID about 12 hours apart) and the DOAC missed-dose rule, and clarify the warfarin-vs-DOAC distinctions (no routine INR, no vitamin K restriction)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Correct dose + missed-dose + monitoring/diet distinctions prevent the most common errors. RN role: regimen + distinctions.

opts.text: Teach drug/food interactions — STOP NSAIDs (use acetaminophen), avoid antiplatelets/other anticoagulants, and call provider before any new med/supplement (CYP3A4 + P-gp)
opts.rat: #3 — Eliminates the highest-yield bleeding interactions. RN role: NSAID stop + interactions.

opts.text: Teach bleeding precautions and signs of bleeding to report (overt + possible internal) and provide a medical-alert ID and wallet card
opts.rat: #4 — Catches and acts on bleeding early; identifies her to emergency teams. RN role: precautions + reporting + ID.

opts.text: Teach perioperative coordination, reversal awareness (andexanet alfa), follow-up labs, cost/refill plan, and verify with teach-back
opts.rat: #5 — Sustains safe long-term care across settings. RN role: perioperative + reversal + follow-up + teach-back.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this DOAC teaching.

opts.text: Take apixaban 5 mg PO BID about 12 hours apart, with or without food, with a pill organizer and reminder to support adherence
opts.rat: Correct dose + adherence supports stroke prevention. RN role: regimen + adherence.

opts.text: Apply the DOAC missed-dose rule (same day = take when remembered; close to next dose = skip; never double)
opts.rat: Prevents bleeding from doubling and loss of protection from chronic doubling. RN role: missed-dose teaching.

opts.text: Stop routine NSAIDs and aspirin/other antiplatelets unless specifically directed; use acetaminophen for routine pain
opts.rat: Eliminates the most common modifiable bleeding interaction. RN role: stop NSAIDs.

opts.text: Call the provider or pharmacist BEFORE starting any new prescription, OTC product, or herbal/supplement (CYP3A4 + P-gp interactions)
opts.rat: Prevents level shifts up (bleeding) or down (clot/stroke). RN role: pre-emptive interaction check.

opts.text: Eat a normal diet with NO vitamin K restriction (correct DOAC teaching, not warfarin)
opts.rat: Avoids unnecessary restriction and the warfarin over-teaching error. RN role: correct diet teaching.

opts.text: Use a soft toothbrush + electric razor, avoid contact sports, carry a medical-alert ID and a wallet card listing apixaban, and report signs of bleeding (including severe headache, sudden weakness/vision change, abdominal/back pain)
opts.rat: Bleeding precautions + reporting + ID-for-emergency. RN role: precautions + reporting + ID.

opts.text: Inform all providers/dentists/surgeons/ED that she takes apixaban; do not stop the medication without provider direction; planned procedures use a provider-coordinated hold based on bleed risk and renal function
opts.rat: Coordinated holds prevent both stroke and bleeding. RN role: perioperative coordination.

opts.text: Take ibuprofen daily for arthritis and a baby aspirin 'for her heart' on top of the apixaban
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — combined antiplatelet/NSAID + DOAC sharply increases bleeding risk; switch to acetaminophen and avoid aspirin unless explicitly directed.

opts.text: Double the next dose to make up for any missed dose of apixaban
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — doubling causes bleeding; if close to the next dose, skip and resume the schedule.

opts.text: Strictly avoid all green leafy vegetables (spinach, kale, broccoli) because they will counteract apixaban
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — vitamin K restriction applies to warfarin, not to DOACs; this is an unnecessary restriction.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Teach the WHY (NVAF + CHA2DS2-VASc 4 → lifelong stroke prevention) and frame adherence as non-negotiable
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Without the WHY, adherence will not hold. RN role: WHY + adherence frame.

opts.text: Teach the regimen (5 mg PO BID about 12 hours apart), the DOAC missed-dose rule, and the warfarin-vs-DOAC distinctions (no routine INR, no vitamin K restriction)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Correct administration is the next-largest impact. RN role: regimen + distinctions.

opts.text: Teach interactions — STOP NSAIDs (use acetaminophen), avoid antiplatelets/other anticoagulants, and call before any new med/supplement (CYP3A4 + P-gp)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Eliminates the highest-yield bleeding interactions. RN role: NSAID stop + interactions.

opts.text: Teach bleeding precautions and signs to report; arrange a medical-alert ID and wallet card noting apixaban
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Detects and acts on bleeding early; identifies her in emergencies. RN role: precautions + reporting + ID.

opts.text: Teach perioperative coordination, reversal awareness (andexanet alfa), follow-up labs, cost/refill plan, and verify with teach-back; involve family
opts.rat: #5 — Closes the loop and sustains safe care over time. RN role: perioperative + reversal + follow-up + teach-back.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: She demonstrates a pill organizer + reminders and on teach-back correctly states the regimen, missed-dose rule, NSAID stop, and when to call 911
opts.rat: Verified understanding + adherence plan. The hallmark of safe DOAC self-management.

opts.text: She uses acetaminophen for pain and reports any unusual bleeding promptly; periodic CBC and renal/hepatic labs remain stable
opts.rat: Correct interaction avoidance + early reporting + stable labs. Expected trajectory.

opts.text: She informs her dentist and any new provider that she takes apixaban; a planned procedure is coordinated with a provider-directed hold and resumed safely
opts.rat: Coordinated perioperative management avoiding both bleeding and stroke. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: At follow-up she remains adherent, in normal sinus rhythm or rate-controlled AFib, with no bleeding events and no stroke
opts.rat: Sustained protection without harm. Ideal long-term outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she stops apixaban on her own because she 'feels fine' and has an embolic ischemic stroke
opts.rat: ⚠️ Self-discontinuation lost stroke protection. Demonstrates why AFib (often asymptomatic) requires lifelong, uninterrupted anticoagulation.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she continues daily ibuprofen for arthritis on top of apixaban and develops a major upper-GI bleed
opts.rat: ⚠️ NSAID + DOAC combination caused major bleeding. Demonstrates why NSAIDs are stopped and acetaminophen substituted.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she doubled the dose after missing one and developed unusual bruising and a nosebleed that would not stop
opts.rat: ⚠️ Doubling caused bleeding. Demonstrates the correct missed-dose rule: take same day; otherwise skip; never double.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she did not tell her dentist about apixaban; uncontrolled bleeding occurred after an extraction
opts.rat: ⚠️ Missing perioperative coordination caused preventable bleeding. Demonstrates informing all providers and coordinating holds.

opts.text: In the first weeks, she takes apixaban correctly with the help of her pill organizer and family support while her routine is still being established
opts.rat: An expected stable baseline while the routine is integrated; continue reinforcement and reassess at follow-up. Unchanged is appropriate here.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she strictly avoids all leafy greens, believing the warfarin diet applies to her DOAC; nutrition narrows without any added safety
opts.rat: ⚠️ Unnecessary restriction with no benefit (and worse nutrition). Demonstrates that DOACs have no vitamin K dietary restriction.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
